topic: A person learns their childhood bully became a therapist
raw_output:
[story 1]

The leather armchair smelled faintly of old paper and expensive soap. Elias sat across from Dr. Thorne, his hands folded neatly in his lap, watching the therapist meticulously adjust a notepad. It had been five years since Mark had been the shadow that loomed over him in the hallways of middle school, the one whose sharp words had carved deep, ugly grooves into Elias’s self-perception. Now, Mark sat there, radiating a quiet, practiced calm that felt alien and utterly unearned. "So," Dr. Thorne began, his voice a low, even murmur, "tell me about the pressure you felt during that transition period." Elias shifted, the movement stiff, his gaze fixed on a dust mote dancing in the afternoon light filtering through the blinds. He offered a clipped, almost monotone summary of events, the words tasting like ash on his tongue. He didn't look up when Mark finally spoke, his voice smooth, modulated—a voice that used to bark insults. "It was... difficult," Elias managed, the admission barely a whisper, a concession made without any real investment.

[story 2]

I kept my shoulders hunched, a familiar armor against the world, even in this sterile, beige office. Mark, now Dr. Thorne, was explaining cognitive reframing techniques. His gestures were precise, his expression one of detached empathy that made my stomach clench with a strange mixture of dread and weariness. Every time he leaned forward, his shadow seemed to engulf me. I found myself tracing the pattern on the worn carpet with my eyes, counting the threads, a pointless exercise in focus. When he asked me to articulate my core anxieties, the words caught in my throat, thick and unformed. I simply stared at the framed certificate on his wall, the polished wood reflecting a distorted image of myself—a stranger wearing the face of someone I used to actively try to erase. I nodded slowly, a minimal acknowledgment, letting the silence stretch until it felt heavy enough to press me into the chair.

[story 3]

The scent of lavender hung heavy in the air, a deliberate attempt at soothing that somehow felt suffocating. I watched Mark across the mahogany desk, observing the practiced ease with which he navigated my vulnerability. He spoke of trauma and attachment styles as if they were abstract concepts, yet the way he spoke about my specific insecurities—the ones he’d once weaponized—made the air crackle with an uncomfortable familiarity. I felt a deep settling in my chest, a kind of finality. There was no fight left in me; the energy required to resist this sudden, unexpected familiarity had long since evaporated. I picked at a loose thread on my sleeve, my movements slow, deliberate, as if conserving every ounce of remaining strength. "Yes," I said finally, the sound flat, devoid of inflection. "That sounds like a lot of work."
